items.q: Why is high IQ a problem for the statistical infrequency definition?
items.correct: It is statistically rare but not abnormal/undesirable.
items.misconception: Because high IQ is actually common.
items.why: Students miss that statistical infrequency lacks a value judgement - it just measures rarity. High IQ is rare but desirable, exposing the definition's limitation.

items.q: What is the capacity of short-term memory according to Miller?
items.why: Students confuse capacity (items) with duration (about 18-30 seconds without rehearsal). Miller's magic number describes how many CHUNKS, not how long.

items.q: According to Maslow, when can self-actualisation be achieved?
items.correct: Only after lower deficiency needs are largely met.
items.misconception: Anyone can self-actualise at any time through willpower.
items.why: Students miss the hierarchical structure. You cannot pursue growth needs while physiological, safety, love, and esteem needs are unmet - that's the whole point of a hierarchy.

items.q: Who proposed the Theory of Planned Behaviour in 1991?
items.why: Correct answer: Ajzen. “Prochaska” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In Prochaska's model, which stage describes thinking about change in the next 6 months?
items.why: Correct answer: Contemplation. “Precontemplation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: How many stages does Prochaska's transtheoretical model have including relapse?
items.why: Exact answer: 6. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
